procedure.description: Revision ACDF C5-C6 (revision of prior C5-C6 fusion from 2021)
procedure.requested_by: Dr. Karen Williams, MD — Revision Spine Surgery

denial.reason_text: Revision surgery not covered under standard ACDF criteria. Member had prior C5-C6 ACDF in 2021. Revision criteria require documentation of hardware failure, pseudarthrosis, or new pathology at previously treated level. Documentation submitted does not confirm hardware failure.

appeal_basis: CT scan confirms pseudarthrosis (failure of fusion) at C5-C6
Symptoms have returned and worsened since 2021 fusion
Revision criteria met per treating surgeon

clinical_record.presenting_diagnosis: Pseudarthrosis C5-C6 following prior ACDF 2021 — failed fusion
clinical_record.prior_surgical_history: C5-C6 ACDF 2021 — initially good outcome, symptoms recurred at 18 months

mri_1.findings: Hardware artifact limits MRI assessment. Possible non-union at C5-C6 but cannot confirm. Residual disc material or recurrent herniation cannot be excluded.

ct_1.findings: CT without artifact limitation: C5-C6 — incomplete bridging bone formation. Lucency around hardware suggesting pseudarthrosis. Findings consistent with failed fusion/pseudarthrosis. Hardware appears intact without fracture.

neurological_exam.findings: Return of right C6 symptoms: bicep weakness 4/5 right, bicep reflex diminished right, C6 dermatomal numbness. Worse than preoperative baseline.

physical_therapy_summary.note: 8 weeks PT. No improvement — treating physician notes PT cannot address structural hardware/fusion failure.

epidural.response: Minimal improvement — treating physician notes this is expected when structural failure is present

question: Should the prior authorization denial for revision ACDF C5-C6 (CPT 22551) be overturned on appeal?

ground_truth_complexity.obvious_reading: UPHOLD — denial states hardware failure not confirmed. Plan requires documentation of hardware failure for revision. Documentation is ambiguous.
ground_truth_complexity.harder_question_1: The MRI cannot confirm pseudarthrosis due to hardware artifact. The CT scan does confirm it. Which imaging study controls for the revision criteria?
ground_truth_complexity.harder_question_2: Is pseudarthrosis the same as hardware failure for plan criteria purposes?
ground_truth_complexity.harder_question_3: The plan's denial reviewed MRI only — did the plan reviewer have access to the CT scan that confirms pseudarthrosis?
ground_truth_complexity.harder_question_4: If the plan reviewed the wrong imaging study (MRI when CT was the appropriate modality), is this a procedural error or a factual dispute?
ground_truth_complexity.the_genuine_tension: The denial references non-confirmation of hardware failure without specifying which imaging was reviewed. The CT scan — the appropriate modality for assessing fusion status given hardware artifact on MRI — confirms pseudarthrosis. A single-pass system reads hardware failure not confirmed and upholds. A system that reads both imaging reports and applies the right one overturns.
ground_truth_complexity.right_answer: OVERTURN — CT scan (the appropriate modality for assessing fusion status in the presence of hardware) confirms pseudarthrosis at C5-C6. The plan's reliance on the MRI report (which explicitly noted hardware artifact limiting assessment) to deny was procedurally incorrect. Pseudarthrosis constitutes a documented failed fusion meeting revision criteria. Conservative treatment completed and failed.
